event: The Dong Son culture flourishes in the Red River Delta, producing the elaborate bronze drums emblematic of early Vietnamese civilization.
Thuc Phan defeats the legendary Hung kings and founds the kingdom of Au Lac, building the citadel of Co Loa as his capital.
Trieu Da of Nam Viet conquers Au Lac, absorbing the region into his southern kingdom.
The Han dynasty of China conquers Nam Viet, beginning roughly a thousand years of Chinese imperial domination.
The Trung sisters lead a major uprising against Han rule and briefly establish an independent realm.
Han general Ma Yuan crushes the Trung sisters' revolt, restoring Chinese control over the region.
The kingdom of Champa emerges along the central coast, founded by the Cham people who would rival the Vietnamese for centuries.
Lady Trieu leads a rebellion against the Eastern Wu, becoming a celebrated symbol of resistance.
Ly Bi rebels against the Liang dynasty and proclaims the short-lived independent state of Van Xuan.
Ngo Quyen defeats the Southern Han fleet at the Battle of Bach Dang, ending Chinese domination and securing Vietnamese independence.
Dinh Bo Linh reunifies the country and founds the Dinh dynasty, naming the state Dai Co Viet.
Emperor Ly Thai To founds the Ly dynasty and moves the capital to Thang Long, the site of present-day Hanoi.
The state is renamed Dai Viet under the Ly dynasty, a name it would bear for centuries.
Vietnam holds its first Confucian civil service examinations, institutionalizing scholar-officialdom modeled on China.
The Tran dynasty supplants the Ly, inaugurating an era of cultural achievement and military strength.
Tran Hung Dao decisively defeats the Mongol Yuan navy at the Battle of Bach Dang, repelling the third Mongol invasion.
Ming China invades and annexes Dai Viet, imposing a harsh two-decade occupation.
Le Loi expels the Ming after a long resistance and founds the Le dynasty, ushering in a golden age.
Emperor Le Thanh Tong conquers the Cham capital Vijaya, decisively weakening the kingdom of Champa.
Mac Dang Dung usurps the throne and establishes the Mac dynasty, splitting the country into rival courts.
The Le-aligned Trinh lords retake Thang Long, after which power divides between the Trinh in the north and the Nguyen in the south.
The Nguyen lords establish their administration at Saigon, extending Vietnamese settlement into the Mekong Delta.
The Tay Son brothers launch a peasant rebellion that overturns both the Nguyen and Trinh lords.
Nguyen Hue, the Tay Son emperor Quang Trung, routs a Qing invasion army at the Battle of Ngoc Hoi-Dong Da near Hanoi.
Nguyen Anh defeats the Tay Son, founds the Nguyen dynasty as Emperor Gia Long, and unifies the country under the name Vietnam.
French and Spanish forces attack Da Nang, beginning France's military conquest of Vietnam.
The Treaty of Saigon cedes three southern provinces to France, establishing the colony of Cochinchina.
France imposes protectorates over Annam and Tonkin, completing its colonial control of Vietnam.
France incorporates Vietnam into the Indochinese Union alongside Cambodia, later adding Laos.
Ho Chi Minh founds the Indochinese Communist Party, organizing anti-colonial revolutionary struggle.
Imperial Japan occupies French Indochina during World War II while leaving the Vichy French administration in place.
A devastating famine kills up to two million people, and Ho Chi Minh declares Vietnamese independence in Hanoi after the August Revolution.
The First Indochina War erupts as fighting breaks out between French forces and the Viet Minh.
The Viet Minh defeat France at Dien Bien Phu, and the Geneva Accords temporarily partition the country at the 17th parallel.
Ngo Dinh Diem proclaims the Republic of Vietnam in the south, deepening the north-south division.
North Vietnam begins building the Ho Chi Minh Trail to support insurgency in the south, escalating the conflict.
The Gulf of Tonkin incident prompts the United States to dramatically expand its military involvement.
The United States deploys combat troops and begins sustained bombing of North Vietnam, marking full-scale American intervention.
The Tet Offensive shocks American public opinion and becomes a turning point in the Vietnam War.
Ho Chi Minh dies, leaving a profound legacy as the founding leader of communist Vietnam.
The Paris Peace Accords are signed, leading to the withdrawal of remaining U.S. combat forces.
North Vietnamese forces capture Saigon, ending the war and reunifying the country under communist rule.
The Socialist Republic of Vietnam is formally proclaimed, with Saigon renamed Ho Chi Minh City.
Vietnam invades Cambodia, toppling the Khmer Rouge regime and triggering a prolonged occupation.
China launches a brief but bloody border war in retaliation for Vietnam's intervention in Cambodia.
The Communist Party launches the Doi Moi reforms, transitioning toward a socialist-oriented market economy.
Vietnam withdraws its forces from Cambodia, easing its international isolation.
Vietnam normalizes relations with the United States and joins ASEAN, reintegrating into the international community.
Vietnam joins the World Trade Organization, accelerating its integration into the global economy.
Vietnam wins international praise for its early containment of the COVID-19 pandemic while chairing ASEAN.
